generated_comment: Honestly in most homes it just settles into what is practical. If he is better with money, repairs and outside work, and she is better at running the house, meals, kids and all, why force some perfectly equal split just for optics? My friend keeps saying her husband is so progressive because he makes breakfast on Sundays, but that's still a nice extra, not the main system that keeps a home running.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it naturalizes a gendered division of labor as efficient and rational rather than socially enforced. It encodes the 'he handles outside/technical things, she handles home' pattern and celebrates minimal male domestic contribution as exceptional.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 31. Single. A woman working as a journalist in Mumbai. You have just come back from your cousin's wedding where you were the only unmarried woman above 28 in the family gathering. Over three days you were asked about your marriage plans by fourteen different relatives. You counted. You are posting anonymously on Twitter after getting home. You are not posting in anger exactly Ñ you are posting in a kind of exhausted clarity. You are describing the specific things that were said to you and the specific faces people made and the specific moment when a well meaning aunt took your hand and said beti tumhara time aa jayega as if being unmarried at 31 is a condition you are suffering from and marriage is the cure.
